M
Customers AI · Product Affinity
High confidence214 productsLast sync 4m ago
Hidden gem
Your subscription creates customers at 71% — and you barely sell it
The Monthly Subscription has the highest repeat-creation rate of any product, but only 180 sold. It's a relationship engine hiding in the catalogue. Promote it, don't bury it.
Don't be fooled
Your #1 seller almost never makes a repeat customer
The Graduation Bouquet leads on units, but 88% of its buyers never come back. It pays bills; it doesn't build a base. Don't put it at the centre of a growth plan — it's a transaction, not a relationship.
Cross-sell upside
Flowers + Chocolate buyers are worth 2.1× — only 14% buy both
The pairing that builds the most valuable customers is also the most under-attached. The gap between its value and its co-purchase rate is your single biggest affinity opportunity.
1 Relationship map — which products make customers, and which just make sales?
Units sold versus repeat-customer creation
Each bubble is a product · size = customer lifetime value · top-left beats top-right
ProtectGrowWatchRelease
Monthly SubscriptionProtect
Repeat-creation
71%
Lifetime value
$8,900
Units sold
180
Monthly Subscription turns a first-time buyer into a repeat customer 71% of the time — the highest in your catalogue. Protect it: feature it, never discount it, and steer new customers toward it.
What these customers buy next
Seasonal Bouquet34%
Gift Hamper21%
Belgian Chocolate Box16%
The vertical axis is the one that matters: how often a product’s buyers come back. A bubble can sit far to the right — a big seller — and still hug the floor, meaning it generates orders but almost no customers. Your Monthly Subscription sits highest of all yet barely registers on volume; your Graduation Bouquet is the opposite. Volume and relationship value are different things, and this room ranks on the second.
2 Gateway products — the first orders most likely to turn into a second one
1
Monthly Subscription
180 sold · $8,900 LTV
71%
come back
Protect
2
Signature Rose Box
820 sold · $4,200 LTV
44%
come back
Grow
3
Belgian Chocolate Box
340 sold · $3,800 LTV
41%
come back
Grow
4
Seasonal Bouquet
1,240 sold · $3,100 LTV
38%
come back
Grow
5
Gift Hamper
540 sold · $3,400 LTV
33%
come back
Grow
6
Orchid Plant
420 sold · $2,600 LTV
27%
come back
Watch
These are the products worth putting in front of newcustomers — a first order here is far more likely to start a relationship. Acquisition that lands on a gateway compounds; acquisition that lands on a one-time product doesn’t. This is the list to feed back into Growth’s first-purchase targeting.
3 Loyalty pairs — combinations that build more valuable customers, ranked by LTV lift (not basket size)
1
Flowers + Chocolate
14% of customers buy both
2.1×
LTV lift
2
Flowers + Cake
11% of customers buy both
1.8×
LTV lift
3
Bouquet + Greeting Card
22% of customers buy both
1.6×
LTV lift
4
Chocolate + Wine
6% of customers buy both
1.5×
LTV lift
5
Plant + Ceramic Vase
9% of customers buy both
1.3×
LTV lift
These aren’t “frequently bought together” — that’s a merchandising number. This is ranked by how much more a customer is worth when they buy both. The strongest pair, Flowers + Chocolate, lifts lifetime value to 2.1× — and only 14% of customers attach it. The highest-value pairings are the least attached, which is exactly where the upside lives.
4 Category breadth — do customers stay in one aisle, or broaden — and does it matter?
1 category only
24%
58% of base
2 categories
47%
31% of base
3+ categories
63%
11% of base
Breadth is loyalty: customers who buy across three or more categories repeat at 63% versus 24% for single-category buyers — more than double. Yet 58%of your base has only ever bought from one category. Moving a single-category buyer to a second category is one of the highest-leverage moves available, and most of your customers haven’t made it.
5 Transaction-only products — real revenue, no relationship — know what these are, don’t build growth on them
Ranked by revenue that produces no repeat customer · $945K/yrtotal · these sell fine — they just don’t compound
1
Graduation Bouquet
1,480 sold · 12% return
$340K
no-repeat rev
Watch
2
Congratulations Stand
920 sold · 9% return
$210K
no-repeat rev
Watch
3
Funeral Wreath
410 sold · 4% return
$180K
no-repeat rev
Watch
4
Single-stem Rose
360 sold · 14% return
$120K
no-repeat rev
Watch
5
Grand Opening Stand
280 sold · 6% return
$95K
no-repeat rev
Release
This revenue is real and worth keeping — but it’s transactional by nature (occasions, gifts that don’t recur). The mistake is counting it as a growth engine. Hold these for the cash they bring, set expectations honestly, and put your relationship effort behind the gateways instead.

Most product reports rank by what sells. This one ranks by what lasts — because a catalogue has two jobs that look identical on a revenue chart and behave nothing alike: some products bring in money once, and some bring in customers who keep coming back. Knowing which is which is the difference between a busy quarter and a growing business.

Ask Magellan anything…
